label: Research
description: Type any word or sentence you're curious about — get real, everyday example sentences and practice repeating them.

label: Custom Scenario
description: Describe any situation you want to practice — a job interview, a landlord dispute, ordering at a specific restaurant — and get an instant custom roleplay.

label: Beginner
description: Everyday survival English. Short sentences, present/past tense, common vocabulary.

scenarios.title: Introducing yourself
story: Hi! My name is Anna.
I am your new neighbor.
I moved here last week.
I have a small dog.
His name is Max.
I like to walk in the park every morning.

scenarios.title: Ordering at a cafe
story: Welcome to Sunny Cafe!
We open at seven every morning.
Today we have fresh croissants.
The coffee is very strong here.
Many people come before work.
I have worked here for two years.

scenarios.title: Asking for directions
story: I live near the train station.
It is not far from here.
I walk there every day.
The station has a big clock outside.
Sometimes the train is late.
I always leave home early.

scenarios.title: Buying groceries
story: Hello! Did you find everything today?
That will be twelve dollars.
Do you have a store card?
You can save money with a card.
Would you like a bag?
Thank you, have a nice day!

scenarios.title: Checking in at a clinic
story: Hello! Do you have an appointment today?
Please write your name here.
The doctor will see you soon.
Please take a seat and wait.
It will not be long.
Thank you for your patience.

scenarios.title: Ordering food by phone
scenarios.character: restaurant staff taking a phone order
story: Thank you for calling Golden Dragon.
What would you like to order?
We have chicken, rice, and noodles.
Delivery takes about thirty minutes.
Can I have your address, please?
Your total is twenty dollars.

scenarios.title: Meeting a new classmate
scenarios.character: a classmate in your English class
story: Hi! Is this seat free?
I am new in this class.
My name is Leo.
I am from Brazil.
What is your name?
Nice to meet you!

scenarios.title: At the post office
story: Hello! How can I help you today?
Do you want to send this package?
Where is it going?
It will take five days.
That will be eight dollars.
Here is your receipt.

scenarios.title: Small talk with a taxi driver
story: Good morning! Where are you going today?
The traffic is bad this morning.
I have driven a taxi for ten years.
Do you live near here?
We will arrive in ten minutes.
Have a good day!

label: Intermediate
description: Natural conversational flow, connected speech, common idioms, opinions and small talk.

scenarios.title: Small talk with a coworker
scenarios.character: a coworker chatting near the coffee machine
story: So, I finally got around to cleaning out my garage this weekend.
It took way longer than I expected.
I found a bunch of old photos from college.
It was such a blast from the past.
My partner kept teasing me for getting distracted every five minutes.
In the end, we filled up three whole boxes to donate.
Honestly, it felt great to clear out the clutter.

scenarios.title: Booking a doctor's appointment by phone
story: We've been absolutely swamped here all week.
Dr. Patel is fully booked until Thursday.
A few patients have had to reschedule because of the flu going around.
Normally we can fit people in the same day.
This week has just been the exception.
I'd recommend booking early next time to be safe.
Hopefully things calm down after the weekend.

scenarios.title: Discussing a movie or show
scenarios.character: a friend who just watched a popular show
story: I finally watched that new thriller everyone's been talking about.
Honestly, the first half really dragged for me.
But the twist at the end completely caught me off guard.
I ended up binge-watching the whole series in one weekend.
My roommate thought I'd lost my mind staying up so late.
Looking back, it was totally worth the lack of sleep.

scenarios.title: Planning a weekend trip with a friend
story: Hey, I was thinking we should get away for the weekend.
I've had my eye on that little mountain town for a while now.
The drive is only a couple of hours, so it's not a big commitment.
We could just wing it and figure out plans once we're there.
Apparently the hiking trails are gorgeous this time of year.
Let's split the cost of a cabin so it doesn't break the bank.
I'm honestly counting down the days already.

scenarios.title: Returning a faulty item at a store
story: I see you're looking to return this — what seems to be the issue?
Ah, that model has had a few hiccups like that reported.
No worries, we can sort this out pretty easily.
Do you have the receipt, or was this a card purchase?
I can offer you a full refund or a straight swap for a new one.
Sorry for the hassle — I know that's frustrating.
It should just take a couple of minutes to process.

scenarios.title: Talking about a new gym routine
story: So, I finally committed to going to the gym four times a week.
The first few sessions nearly wiped me out completely.
But I'm slowly getting the hang of the routine now.
My trainer keeps pushing me to up the weights each week.
Honestly, I was skeptical at first, but I'm seeing real progress.
The hardest part is just dragging myself there after work.
Once I'm in the zone though, I don't want to stop.

scenarios.title: Discussing a travel delay
scenarios.character: a fellow traveler at the airport
story: Looks like we're stuck here for a while — the storm really threw things off.
I've been sitting at this gate for almost three hours now.
Apparently a bunch of connecting flights got backed up because of this.
I was supposed to make it home in time for a family dinner tonight.
At this point, I'm just trying to make the best of it.
At least the airport has decent food around here.
Fingers crossed they announce a new departure time soon.

scenarios.title: Catching up after a long time
story: Wow, it's been way too long since we last caught up!
I feel like so much has changed since we last spoke.
I actually ended up switching careers a couple of years back.
It was a huge leap of faith, but it paid off in the end.
How about you — what have you been up to all this time?
We really should make more of an effort to keep in touch.
Let's grab coffee properly sometime soon.

scenarios.title: Talking about a recipe
story: I tried out a new recipe last night and it turned out surprisingly well.
It's this one-pot pasta dish I found online.
I always thought one-pot meals would taste bland, but I was totally wrong.
The trick is letting everything simmer together for a good while.
I ended up making way too much, so I've been eating leftovers all week.
You should definitely give it a shot sometime.
I can send you the recipe if you're interested.

label: Advanced
description: Nuance, persuasion, debate, storytelling, and idiomatic, native-like phrasing.

scenarios.title: Friendly debate
scenarios.character: a friend who mildly disagrees about remote work
story: Look, I get why people romanticize working from home, but hear me out.
Ever since we went remote, I feel like half my day gets swallowed up by messages that could've been a two-minute chat.
There's something about being in the same room that just cuts through the noise.
Sure, the commute was a drag, but at least it gave me a clear line between work and home.
Now I catch myself answering emails at ten at night, and that's not healthy.
I'm not saying offices are perfect, but this whole remote-work honeymoon phase might be wearing off for a lot of people.
At the end of the day, I think a hybrid setup splits the difference best.

scenarios.title: Job interview - tough questions
story: Let me tell you about a hire that really put our team through the wringer last year.
We brought someone on who looked fantastic on paper, but the culture fit just wasn't there.
Within a month, tensions were boiling over between departments.
I had to step in and mediate more times than I'd like to admit.
Eventually we made the tough call to part ways, which was a bitter pill to swallow for everyone involved.
It taught me that skills alone don't cut it if someone can't read the room.
Since then, we've leaned much harder on behavioral interviews like this one.

scenarios.title: Telling a story with follow-ups
scenarios.character: a friend recounting a solo trip
story: A few years ago I decided to backpack through Vietnam completely solo.
I'll admit, the first few days were nerve-wracking since I barely spoke a word of the language.
But the locals went out of their way to help me whenever I looked lost.
I ended up joining a group of travelers I met at a hostel in Hanoi.
We rented scooters and rode along the coast for nearly a week straight.
At one point we got caught in a massive storm and had to wait it out in a tiny roadside shack.
Looking back, that random storm turned into one of my favorite memories of the whole trip.

scenarios.title: Debating social media's impact
story: I really think social media has done more harm than good at this point.
Everyone's constantly comparing their behind-the-scenes to everyone else's highlight reel.
It's warping how people, especially teenagers, see themselves.
Sure, it's connected people across distances in ways we never had before.
But I can't ignore how much anxiety it seems to be fueling.
The algorithms are designed to be addictive on purpose, and that worries me.
At some point, we have to ask who's actually benefiting from all this engagement.
What's your take — are we overblowing the downsides?

scenarios.title: Negotiating a salary raise
story: I appreciate you bringing this up directly with me.
You've clearly put in the work this past year, and that hasn't gone unnoticed.
That said, budget approvals this cycle have been tighter than usual.
I want to make sure whatever we land on is something I can actually defend upstairs.
Let's talk through your accomplishments so I have concrete points to work with.
I can't promise the exact number you're after, but I'm willing to advocate for you.
Give me a few days to see what flexibility we actually have.

scenarios.title: Discussing a controversial book
story: I have to say, this book completely divided our group.
Some people found the ending brilliant, almost inevitable in hindsight.
Others felt like it completely betrayed everything set up earlier.
I personally think the author was deliberately leaving things unresolved.
It's the kind of book that lingers with you long after you finish it.
Still, I can see why some readers felt cheated out of closure.
What stuck with you most after finishing it?

scenarios.title: Giving constructive criticism to a friend
story: Hey, I wanted to bring something up, and I hope you take it in the spirit it's meant.
I've noticed you've been canceling plans pretty last-minute lately.
I get that things come up, but it's started to feel like a pattern.
I'm not trying to guilt-trip you, I just miss actually seeing you.
I'd rather say something now than let it quietly build resentment.
I know you've got a lot on your plate right now, and I want to be understanding.
I just wanted to be upfront instead of letting it fester.

scenarios.title: Persuading a skeptical friend to invest
story: I know you're generally pretty risk-averse, so hear me out on this one.
This isn't some get-rich-quick scheme I stumbled onto overnight.
I've been researching this for months before even considering putting money in.
The returns won't be overnight, but the fundamentals seem genuinely solid.
I get why you'd be skeptical, given how many of these pitches turn out to be scams.
I'm not asking you to go all in, just to keep an open mind.
Worst case, you do a bit more digging and decide it's not for you.

scenarios.title: Reflecting on a career change
story: Leaving that job was probably the scariest decision I've ever made.
Everyone around me thought I was throwing away a stable career.
For months, I genuinely second-guessed whether I'd made a huge mistake.
But looking back now, I don't think I could've stayed much longer without burning out completely.
The transition period was rough, financially and mentally.
These days, I actually look forward to Mondays, which felt impossible before.
I guess the lesson is that comfort and fulfillment aren't always the same thing.

label: Business & Accounting English
description: Meetings, presenting numbers, client calls, negotiation, and finance vocabulary.

scenarios.title: Leading a status meeting
scenarios.character: a colleague joining your project status meeting
story: So, quick rundown on where we stand this quarter.
We hit our revenue target, but just barely, coming in at around ninety-eight percent of forecast.
The delay on the vendor contract pushed a few deliverables into next month.
On the plus side, the new client onboarding process cut our ramp-up time nearly in half.
We did go over budget slightly on contractor costs, mostly due to the platform migration.
Overall, I'd call it a solid quarter with a couple of lessons learned.

scenarios.title: Presenting quarterly numbers
story: Let's walk through the numbers from last quarter.
Revenue came in at 2.4 million, up eight percent from the previous quarter.
Operating expenses rose slightly due to increased marketing spend.
Our gross margin held steady at around forty-two percent.
The biggest variance was in travel costs, which came in well under budget.
Overall, net income landed close to our forecast, with only a small shortfall.

scenarios.title: Client call about an invoice
scenarios.character: a client asking about a discrepancy
story: I was going through last month's invoice and noticed a charge I didn't recognize.
It's listed as a service fee, but I don't remember discussing that upfront.
My accounting team flagged it during our reconciliation process.
I want to make sure we're not being billed for something outside our agreement.
This isn't the first time we've had a small discrepancy like this.
I'd like to get it sorted before it affects our next payment cycle.

scenarios.title: Negotiating a budget
scenarios.character: a department head questioning your proposal
story: I've looked over your budget proposal, and a few line items are giving me pause.
The increase in software licensing costs seems steep compared to last year.
I understand the team has grown, but the numbers don't quite add up for me yet.
We're under pressure company-wide to tighten spending this cycle.
I'd need a clearer breakdown before I can justify this increase to finance.
That said, I'm open to finding a middle ground if the case is strong enough.

scenarios.title: Explaining a tax deadline to a client
story: I wanted to check in about the upcoming filing deadline.
I'm a little worried we don't have all the documentation ready yet.
Is there any flexibility if we need a short extension?
I don't want to risk any penalties on our end.
What exactly would you need from us to move forward this week?
I'd rather sort this out now than scramble at the last minute.

scenarios.title: Discussing cash flow problems
story: I need to flag something concerning about our cash position this month.
A few large clients have pushed their payments out past the usual terms.
That's put us in a tighter spot than the forecast anticipated.
We might need to delay some of the discretionary spending for now.
I've already reached out to a couple of the slower-paying accounts.
If this continues next month, we'll need to revisit our credit line.
I wanted to loop you in before it becomes a bigger issue.

scenarios.title: Presenting an audit finding
story: During our review, we identified a discrepancy in the expense reporting process.
Several transactions were missing proper supporting documentation.
This isn't necessarily an indication of wrongdoing, but it is a control gap.
We'd recommend tightening the approval workflow going forward.
I'll include the specific line items in our formal report.
Do you have any context that might explain these particular entries?

scenarios.title: Onboarding a new hire on expense policy
story: Thanks for walking me through this — I want to make sure I get it right.
So just to confirm, receipts are required for anything over twenty-five dollars?
What's the turnaround time for reimbursement once I submit a report?
Are client meals treated differently from regular team lunches?
I appreciate you being patient with all these questions.
I'd rather ask now than submit something incorrectly later.

scenarios.title: Handling a vendor payment dispute
story: I'm following up regarding the invoice that's now significantly overdue.
I understand delays happen, but this has gone past our agreed terms twice now.
We value the relationship, which is why I wanted to raise it directly rather than escalate.
Is there something on your end causing the holdup with processing?
We'd appreciate a clear timeline so we can plan accordingly.
I'm happy to work with you on a revised schedule if needed.
